published.text:
### Added section
Heading: When is consultant help worth paying for first?
Body: Most small businesses hit a point where the cost of not automating is higher than the cost of hiring help. Here are the four scenarios where paying a consultant now saves more than waiting.

### Scenario cards
1. Leads are going cold because nobody responds fast enough
- If your average response time is hours or days, every week you wait is revenue you already paid to generate walking out the door. A consultant scopes and ships a lead-response system in days, not months of trial-and-error.
- Links: /best-ai-automation-for-service-businesses, /ai-automation-consultant-pricing-small-business

2. You tried DIY tools and the workflow broke or stalled
- A half-built Zapier chain or abandoned n8n flow means you already spent the time and still have the problem. A consultant picks up where you stopped, fixes what's broken, and finishes the job with a system that stays running.
- Links: /hire-ai-automation-consultant-vs-diy, /case-studies

3. You have no technical staff and can't debug integrations
- When nobody on the team can troubleshoot API errors or broken triggers, every outage becomes a fire drill. A consultant builds the system, documents it, and hands off something your non-technical team can actually operate.
- Links: /ai-automation-for-businesses-without-a-tech-team, /ai-automation-implementation-roadmap

4. You're about to hire for a role that automation could replace
- If the job description is mostly data entry, follow-up, or scheduling, a one-time automation build often costs less than two months of salary — and runs 24/7 without onboarding or turnover.
- Links: /ai-automation-consultant-pricing-small-business, /best-ai-automation-for-service-businesses

edits.description: This was a bounded indexing-support refresh for a discovered-not-indexed commercial page, not a new page launch.
The new block is written as a buyer-question answer, not generic navigation, so it can stand alone in search or AI extraction.
Each scenario names a concrete small-business trigger in plain language: slow response, stalled DIY, no technical staff, or a pending admin hire.
Internal links stay tightly clustered around adjacent high-intent pages that already exist live: pricing, proof, hire-vs-DIY, no-tech-team, implementation roadmap, and best-first-automation.
Tone stays practical and cost-aware. The section argues for consultant help only when delay, fragility, or headcount cost already make the economics obvious.
